subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear skeleton with headings (Introduction, Basics, Coordinate Transformations, Conclusion) provides basic organization
Roughly chronological narrative allows reader to follow development from linear webs to parabolas to rotated web
Report stays mostly on topic, focusing on mathematical modelling of spider webs
weaknesses: Transitions between sections are sometimes abrupt and explanatory gaps remain (e.g. choice of 1â6 scaling)
Multiple figures are uncited or lack labels/units, weakening coherence
Redundant narrative on spider appeal and conclusion placed before final results dilute conciseness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments consistently placed Presentation in band 2: organisation is adequate but unpolished, coherence is partial with some digressions, and conciseness is mostly maintained yet contains unnecessary material. A score of 2 reflects these middling strengths and notable weaknesses.

strengths: Use of standard symbols (xâ², yâ², Î¸, sin, cos) and an overview of rotation shows clear mathematical language in places
Algebraic derivations are shown stepwise, supporting logical argument in sections
Inclusion of graphs and Desmos screenshots gives some multiple representations
weaknesses: Notation is inconsistent (e.g. âaâ vs âÎ±â, missing LaTeX commands for functions)
Graphs are often too small and lack axis labels/units, limiting interpretability
Some derivations jump steps and lack formal domain restrictions or tables
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands of Mathematical Communication were assessed at band 2: some appropriate language and notation, some representations but not always effective, and some logical clarity with occasional gaps. A score of 2 accurately reflects this level of performance.

strengths: Introduction links to the studentâs personal fascination with spiders, adding authenticity
Choice of modelling spider webs rather than a standard problem shows some personal initiative
weaknesses: Independent mathematical insight is limited, as most derivations follow textbook methods
Creativity and initiative are moderate rather than innovative
Personal approach is evident but not developed into original methods or extensions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Independent thinking was judged at band 1 with only limited evidence, while personal approach and creativity sat at band 2. Given the prominence of independent insight in this criterion and overall limited originality, a score of 1 best captures the studentâs engagement.

strengths: Student notes the challenge of replication and expresses admiration for spiders
Some qualitative comments on ânaturalityâ of the model demonstrate initial reflection
weaknesses: No concrete evaluation of mathematical success or parameter sensitivity
Reflection remains at a descriptive level without critical analysis of methods or results
No discussion of next steps, limitations or future implications
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All reflection strands were placed in band 1 for limited depth, basic analysis, and minimal evaluation. A score of 1 aligns with the sparse and descriptive nature of the studentâs reflection.

strengths: Derivation and application of rotation equations are mostly correct and clearly linked to the modelling goal
Use of course-level mathematics (rotation matrices, bounding parabolas, inverse functions, slope) is appropriate and relevant
Graphs correspond with equations, demonstrating good understanding and application
weaknesses: Typographical errors in endpoint signs for bounds (both stated as x = 1.25)
Some intermediate steps (e.g. slope calculation) are omitted, reducing transparency
Notation slips (ambiguous Î», repeated listing of similar parabolas) suggest areas for tightening
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Use of Mathematics was placed at band 3 across all strands: relevant mathematics used appropriately, commensurate course-level content, and good understanding with minor errors. A score of 3 correctly reflects this solid, though not exceptional, mathematical performance.

strandSelections.reasoning: Steps within individual derivations are shown logically, yet later sections jump (e.g. slope calculation is quoted once then four directions asserted).  Arguments are understandable but not consistently rigorous or complete, so clarity is present only âsomeâ of the time â band 2.

Derivation of rotation equations is mostly correct and graphs match equations; minor notation slips are present but few substantive errors.  Understanding appears good but not flawless, so band 3 is suitable.

The exploration includes algebraic derivations, several Desmos screen-shots and one hand-drawn schematic; however there are no tables or alternative representations.  Graphs do support ideas but are sometimes too small to read scales.  This satisfies "some use" rather than "effective use", so band 2 is appropriate.

The student follows a roughly chronological narrative (linear web â parabolas â rotation â final web) so the reader can see how ideas build, but transitions are occasionally abrupt and several explanatory gaps remain (e.g. why the 1â6 scaling was chosen).  Some repetition and digressions about spider-web ânaturalityâ weaken the line of argument, so overall coherence is evident but only partial, consistent with band 2.

Modelling a spider web with bounded rotated parabolas and lines is a creative context for SL mathematics and required some initiative with Desmos.  Nonetheless, creativity is moderate rather than exceptional, meriting band 2.

Analysis of results is rudimentary: the student states the web âlooks more naturalâ without measurable criteria, and no discussion of errors or parameter sensitivity is offered.  Only basic commentary is present, so band 1 is justified.

The student concludes that full replication is possible with âtime and patienceâ but provides no concrete evaluation of mathematical success or future improvements.  Evaluation is therefore limited, corresponding to band 1.

Reflection is confined to brief comments that the task was harder than expected and admiration for spiders.  There is little attempt to question choices or mathematical results.  This limited insight fits band 1.

Standard symbols (xâ², yâ², Î¸, sin, cos) are used and basic algebra is shown, but notation is sometimes informal or incorrect ("$cos(Î¸+a)$" typed without backslash, inconsistent variable âa/Î±â).  Hence appropriate language appears but not consistently, aligning with band 2.

The student clearly links the topic to a personal childhood fascination and selects specific images to imitate, demonstrating a personal route through the task.  The approach, while not innovative, is visibly their own, matching the âclear evidenceâ descriptor of band 2.

At ~1 800 words the report is readable but contains sizeable non-mathematical commentary (e.g. several paragraphs on why webs are unique) and duplicated explanations of bounds.  Consequently it is mostly relevant yet contains noticeable unnecessary material, matching band 2 for conciseness.

All mathematics (rotation matrices, bounding, inverse functions, slope) is pertinent to the modelling goal and used in context, though depth is moderate.  This aligns with ârelevant mathematics used appropriately,â i.e., band 3.

Choosing to investigate spider webs and deriving rotation formula shows a spark of individual choice, but most mathematics follows textbook methods with little original insight.  Evidence of independent thinking is therefore limited, consistent with band 1.

Headings (Introduction, Basics, Coordinate Transformations, Conclusion) give a basic skeleton, yet sub-sections are uneven in length and the conclusion appears mid-page before the bibliography.  Figures are inserted but not always cited in the text.  Thus organisation is adequate but not clearly polished, fitting the descriptor for band 2.

Rotation of axes and manipulation of quadratic forms are on-level for AA SL and applied fairly.  Content does not rise to âfully comprehensiveâ but is certainly commensurate with the course, warranting band 3.

annotations.comment: The introduction effectively links personal fascination with spiders, demonstrating clear personal engagement. This personal context adds authenticity to the exploration.
The aim statement is broad and lacks measurable objectives; refine it by specifying quantifiable goals (e.g., number of rings, rotation angles) to sharpen focus.
Several narrative paragraphs on spidersâ appeal dilute mathematical focus; consider condensing to maintain relevance and improve conciseness.
The definition of âboundsâ is informal; include a precise domain restriction notation (e.g. âf(x)=3 for â1.25â¤xâ¤1.25â) to enhance mathematical communication.
Figure 1 is not cited in the adjacent text. Add a specific reference (e.g. âsee Figure 1â) where the linear web is discussed to improve coherence.
Typographical error: both endpoints are stated as x=1.25; the lower bound should be x=â1.25. Correct this to avoid misunderstanding.
The caption for Figure 2 lacks axis labels and scale indicators. Adding these will improve readerâs ability to interpret the pre-bound graph precisely.
The description of the bounds repeats the same typo of x=1.25 twice; ensure correct sign for lower bound (â1.25) to maintain mathematical accuracy.
Consider formalizing the explanation of bounds using set notation or interval notation to convey domain restrictions more concisely.
Combining linear segments to approximate a web is a solid first step; this progression to parabolas shows good relevance of mathematics to context.
The âAdding Parabolasâ section would benefit from a brief outline of sub-steps to guide the reader through the forthcoming derivations.
Phrasing like ânot as easy as it may soundâ is informal; replace with a concise statement of the algebraic challenges in rotating parabolas.
The overview of rotation is clear and sets a strong foundation for coordinate transformationsâgood use of mathematical language.
Figure 4 omits axis units and labels; include them to clarify measurement of r, Î±, and Î¸ in the schematic.
The inline equation âcos(Î¸+a)â is missing a backslash before âcosâ; ensure all functions use correct LaTeX commands.
The derivation expands using âaâ instead of the earlier symbol âÎ±â, creating inconsistency; standardize notation throughout.
The example âe^{Î»}(â¦)â uses Î» ambiguously. It should explicitly substitute xâ² for the new variable to avoid confusion.
Figure 5 is too small to read scale markings; either enlarge the image or supplement with a table of key rotated parabola values.
Describing âseven more parabolasâ is repetitive; consider summarizing the general rotated parabola equation instead of listing each case.
When discussing the inverse function x=yÂ², note domain restrictions to ensure the inverse remains a function on the chosen interval.
Explanation of the inverse function and rotations is correct but would be strengthened by specifying intersection coordinates for bounding parabolas.
Figure 6 is introduced without in-text reference. Integrate discussion of âPrebound Parabola Webâ before showing the figure.
Figure 7âs axes lack labels and units; adding these will enhance interpretability of the post-bound parabola web.
The âNaturalityâ section notes qualitative flaws but lacks critical reflection on modeling limitations; discuss how gravity or irregularities affect equation choices.
Calling spider webs âuniqueâ is insightful, but reflection would be deepened by proposing quantifiable measures for uniqueness (e.g., angle variance).
Figure 11âs caption duplicates earlier figure numbers and lacks uniqueness; ensure each figure caption is distinct and accurately referenced.
The reference list relies heavily on non-peer-reviewed online articles; consider integrating more academic or textbook sources to support mathematical content.
The description of the triple web replication omits mention of the scaling factor (1/6) used earlier; restate it to maintain mathematical continuity.
The slope formula uses superscripts yÂ²âyÂ¹; conventional notation is yââyâ for clarity. Adjust indices to standard form.
The computed slope M=2.411 is stated without showing intermediate subtraction and division; include these steps to validate accuracy.
Describing construction with slope, its negative, and reciprocals is clear, but explicit equations for these lines would strengthen the mathematical justification.
Selecting a specific natural web for evaluation is good initiative; what criteria (e.g., symmetry, density) guided this choice?
The conclusion paragraph appears before the final figure and reads more like a summary than a structured conclusion; reorganize so conclusion follows all results.
The conclusion offers descriptive remarks but lacks evaluation of mathematical learning and suggestions for improvement; include critical reflection and next steps.
The bibliography entries lack consistent formatting (e.g., punctuation, italics); adopt a standard citation style such as APA or MLA.
The standalone caption âPhoto.â at the end is unclear and unreferenced; remove or integrate it properly with a corresponding figure.
